tasks.sourceTextEn: The notion that scientific knowledge advances through a steady accumulation of facts, a view long cherished by the public, has been seriously challenged by historians of science. What they have come to recognize is that progress in any discipline rarely follows a smooth, linear path; rather, it proceeds through periods of relative stability punctuated by abrupt and often disorienting upheavals. During such moments, the very assumptions that researchers had taken to be self-evident are called into question, and the framework within which problems were once posed gives way to a new one. This pattern, however unsettling it may appear to those who prize certainty, lies at the heart of how human understanding genuinely deepens over time. To grasp this is to abandon the comforting fiction that the road to knowledge runs straight, and to accept that every settled conviction remains, in principle, open to revision.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is often assumed that the rapid spread of digital communication has brought people closer together, yet the reality may be considerably more complicated than this optimistic picture suggests. While individuals can now exchange messages across continents in an instant, the depth of the connections so formed remains open to doubt. Sociologists who study online behaviour have observed that the sheer volume of interaction available to us does not necessarily translate into meaningful relationships; on the contrary, it may foster a kind of restless superficiality, in which attention is perpetually divided. What such observations imply is that technology, for all its undeniable conveniences, cannot by itself supply the patience and presence on which genuine intimacy depends. The tools that promise to abolish distance, in other words, may leave wholly untouched the slower and harder task of truly coming to know another person.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Few institutions have undergone so profound a transformation in recent decades as the modern university. Once conceived primarily as a sanctuary in which scholars might pursue knowledge for its own sake, it has increasingly come to be regarded, by governments and students alike, as an engine of economic productivity whose worth is measured chiefly by the employability of its graduates. This shift, gradual though it has been, raises uncomfortable questions about what education is ultimately for. If the value of learning is to be reckoned solely in terms of the salaries it commands, then those forms of inquiry that yield no immediate financial return are likely to find themselves marginalized, however vital they may be to a thoughtful and humane society.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The relationship between memory and personal identity has long fascinated philosophers, who have struggled to explain how a person can remain the same individual over a lifetime despite the ceaseless flux of experience. Some have argued that it is precisely the continuity of remembered events that binds together the disparate moments of a life into a coherent self. Others, more skeptical, point out that memory is notoriously unreliable, prone to distortion and selective forgetting, and ask how so fragile a faculty could possibly bear the weight of our sense of who we are. What both camps acknowledge, nonetheless, is that without some thread linking past to present, the very idea of a persisting self would dissolve. The puzzle, in the end, may reveal less about memory than about how desperately we need to believe that we are, despite everything, one and the same person throughout.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Among the many consequences of industrialization, none has proved more far-reaching than its impact upon our perception of time. In agrarian societies, the rhythms of daily life had been governed by the natural cycles of sun and season; with the coming of the factory, however, time itself was reconceived as a measurable commodity, to be parcelled out, bought, and sold like any other resource. Workers who had once labored according to the demands of the harvest now found their movements dictated by the relentless ticking of the clock. This profound reordering of human experience, though seldom remarked upon at the time, would come to shape the assumptions and anxieties of generations yet unborn. So thoroughly have we internalized this new discipline that we can scarcely imagine a mode of living in which the hours were not perpetually counted and weighed.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It would be a mistake to suppose that creativity is the exclusive preserve of a gifted few, born with talents the rest of us can only envy. Research in cognitive psychology increasingly suggests that the capacity to generate novel and useful ideas is far more widely distributed than popular myth allows, and that it can be cultivated through deliberate practice no less than any other skill. What distinguishes highly creative individuals, it appears, is not some mysterious spark denied to ordinary minds, but rather a willingness to tolerate uncertainty, to entertain seemingly absurd possibilities, and to persist in the face of repeated failure. Seen in this light, creativity becomes less a matter of innate endowment than of disposition and habit. If this is correct, then the most consequential question is not who happens to possess the gift, but what kinds of environment encourage the rest of us to develop the capacities we already have.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The argument that economic inequality is simply the natural and inevitable outcome of differences in individual effort and ability, frequently advanced by those who benefit most from the existing order, deserves closer examination than it usually receives. Careful study of how wealth is actually transmitted across generations reveals that the circumstances of one's birth exert an influence at least as powerful as anything one subsequently does. A child born into privilege inherits not only material assets but also networks, expectations, and forms of confidence that markedly improve the odds of success. To attribute the resulting disparities solely to merit is therefore to mistake the consequences of accumulated advantage for the rewards of personal virtue. Such a confusion is hardly innocent, for it furnishes the comfortable with a flattering story about why they deserve what they enjoy.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Language, far from being a mere instrument for labelling a reality that exists independently of it, plays an active part in shaping the very categories through which we apprehend the world. This insight, developed by linguists over the course of the past century, implies that speakers of different languages may, in subtle but significant ways, inhabit somewhat different conceptual universes. Where one tongue draws a sharp distinction, another may see only continuity; what one culture treats as a single phenomenon, another may carve into several. Such variation, while it ought not be exaggerated into a claim that translation is impossible, does suggest that the words we inherit quietly predispose us to notice certain things and to overlook others. To learn another language well, on this view, is to acquire not merely a new vocabulary but an unfamiliar way of dividing up experience.

tasks.sourceTextEn: There has emerged in recent years a growing consensus that the traditional distinction between work and leisure, once so firmly drawn, is beginning to erode. Portable devices that keep employees perpetually reachable have blurred the boundary between the office and the home, so that the hours nominally set aside for rest are increasingly colonized by the demands of one's job. While champions of this arrangement praise the flexibility it affords, critics warn that the inability ever fully to disengage takes a quiet but cumulative toll on health and wellbeing. What is at stake, they contend, is nothing less than the capacity to lead a life in which labour, however necessary, does not crowd out everything else that makes existence worthwhile.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The widespread belief that human beings are fundamentally selfish, pursuing their own advantage at every turn, has been thrown into question by a body of research that paints a far more nuanced portrait of our nature. Experiments conducted across many cultures reveal that people routinely act in ways that benefit others at a cost to themselves, even when no reciprocity can be expected and no observer is present to applaud. Such findings do not, of course, deny that self-interest is a powerful motive; they suggest, rather, that it coexists with a deep-seated tendency toward cooperation, fairness, and concern for the wellbeing of strangers. To ignore this second dimension is to misunderstand what kind of creature we actually are. A theory of human conduct built upon the premise of pure selfishness will therefore mispredict precisely those moments in which people behave most admirably.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Historians have increasingly come to appreciate that the events recorded in official documents represent only a fraction of what actually took place in the past. The lives of ordinary men and women, who left behind few written traces of their existence, have for centuries remained largely invisible to scholarship preoccupied with kings, battles, and treaties. In recent decades, however, a new generation of researchers has turned its attention to court records, parish registers, and the fragmentary testimony of the obscure, attempting to reconstruct experiences that conventional history had passed over in silence. What emerges from these patient labours is a richer and more democratic picture of the past, one in which the powerless are at last accorded a voice. History so written ceases to be a chronicle of the mighty and becomes, instead, an account of how the great mass of humanity actually endured.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Whether the pursuit of happiness ought to be regarded as the proper aim of a human life is a question that has divided thinkers since antiquity. Those who answer in the affirmative tend to assume that pleasant feelings are intrinsically good and that a life is improved to the extent that such feelings predominate. Yet a moment's reflection reveals difficulties with this view. Many of the experiences we most value, from raising children to mastering a demanding art, involve considerable hardship, and would scarcely be chosen if comfort were our sole criterion. It may be, then, that a meaningful life and a merely pleasant one are not the same thing, and that we sometimes have good reason to prefer the former.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The conviction that scientific and technological progress will of itself solve the environmental problems it has helped to create rests on assumptions that merit careful scrutiny. It is true that ingenuity has repeatedly devised remedies for difficulties once thought insurmountable, and there is no reason in principle why it should not continue to do so. Nevertheless, to place unbounded faith in future breakthroughs is to risk postponing indefinitely the changes in behaviour and policy that the present moment demands. Technology may indeed buy us time, but the belief that it will rescue us regardless of how we choose to live encourages precisely the complacency that has brought us to our current predicament. Prudence counsels that we treat innovation as an ally to be cultivated rather than a saviour to be awaited.

reference points help scoring; they are not the only correct translation

tasks.sourceTextEn: The assumption that markets, left entirely to themselves, will always allocate resources in the most efficient and socially beneficial manner has been one of the most influential ideas of the modern era. Yet experience suggests that unregulated markets, for all their dynamism, are prone to failures that no amount of competition can correct on its own. They tend systematically to ignore costs imposed on third parties, such as the pollution generated by production, and they frequently undersupply goods, like basic research, whose benefits cannot easily be captured by those who pay for them. Recognizing these limitations need not imply hostility to markets as such; it implies only that they function best within a framework of intelligent rules. The relevant debate, properly understood, concerns not whether markets should be governed but how, and by whom, the necessary rules are to be devised.

keyTerms: allocate resources→配置资源; unregulated markets→不受监管的市场; third parties→第三方; undersupply→供给不足; basic research→基础研究
syntacticFocus: 同位语从句、过去分词独立结构、过去分词后置定语、whose 与 who 定语从句、need not imply 否定

tasks.sourceTextEn: What we call common sense, far from being a reliable guide to truth, often turns out to be little more than the accumulated prejudices of a particular time and place, dressed up in the language of self-evidence. Many propositions that earlier generations regarded as too obvious to require argument, concerning the proper roles of men and women, for instance, or the natural ordering of human societies, strike us today as plainly mistaken. This ought to instil a certain humility about our own convictions. If those who came before us were so confidently wrong about matters they took to be beyond dispute, we have little reason to suppose that we ourselves are wholly exempt from comparable errors. The most dangerous beliefs, after all, are not those we argue for but those we never think to examine at all.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The idea that art should serve some useful social purpose, rather than existing merely for the delight it affords, has provoked fierce controversy among critics and artists alike. On one side stand those who insist that a work of genuine value must engage with the pressing concerns of its time, holding up a mirror to injustice or stirring its audience to action. On the other are defenders of the view that beauty requires no justification beyond itself, and that to demand of art a practical payoff is to misconstrue its very nature. Perhaps the dispute rests on a false opposition; a painting or a poem may move us deeply precisely because it refuses to be reduced to a message. The work that endures is rarely the one that preaches, but rather the one that, by its very form, enlarges our capacity to feel and to perceive.

meaningUnits: 艺术应否服务于有用社会目的而非仅为愉悦引发激烈争论；一方坚持真正有价值的作品须介入时代紧迫关切、映照不公或激发行动；另一方主张美无须自身之外的辩护、要求艺术有实际回报即曲解其本性；或许争论建于虚假对立，画作或诗篇打动我们恰因其拒被化约为讯息

tasks.sourceTextEn: It has become fashionable to speak of the brain as though it were simply a sophisticated computer, processing inputs and generating outputs according to fixed rules. While this metaphor has undeniably proved fruitful, guiding much valuable research, there are reasons to suspect that it captures only a part of the truth. Unlike any machine yet devised, the human mind is embedded in a living body and shaped continuously by emotion, by social relationships, and by a history of experiences that no two individuals share in exactly the same way. To reduce so rich and dynamic a phenomenon to the operations of a mechanism, however complex, may be to leave out what is most essential about it. A metaphor that illuminates one aspect of its subject can, if pressed too far, quietly obscure all the rest.

keyTerms: a sophisticated computer→精密的计算机; metaphor→隐喻; embedded in a living body→嵌于活体之中; mechanism→机制; what is most essential→最本质之物

tasks.sourceTextEn: Few subjects arouse stronger feelings than the question of how much we owe to future generations who do not yet exist and whose interests cannot, by their very nature, be consulted. Some philosophers maintain that since these people are no less real for being unborn, we are bound to take their welfare into account when we make decisions whose consequences will outlast us. Others reply that obligations can be owed only to actual persons, and that it is incoherent to speak of duties toward those who may never come into being at all. However the dispute is resolved, the choices we make about resources and the climate will profoundly shape a world we ourselves shall never see. That those who will inhabit that world cannot reproach us does not, on reflection, make our responsibility toward them any lighter.

keyTerms: future generations→后代/子孙后代; whose interests cannot be consulted→其利益无从征询; no less real→同样真实; obligations→义务; come into being→产生/存在

tasks.sourceTextEn: The claim that travel broadens the mind, repeated so often that it has hardened into a cliché, deserves to be examined rather than simply accepted. There is no doubt that exposure to unfamiliar ways of living can loosen the grip of habits we had never thought to question. Yet travel undertaken in the wrong spirit, as a mere collection of sights to be photographed and ticked off, may leave the traveller's assumptions wholly intact, or even reinforce them. What transforms a journey into an education is not the distance covered but the willingness to be genuinely unsettled by what one encounters, to surrender, however briefly, the comforting conviction that one's own way of life is the natural one. Travel of this rarer kind sends us home, paradoxically, as strangers to the assumptions we had never before thought to question.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is widely supposed that democracy and economic prosperity naturally reinforce one another, the one creating the conditions in which the other can flourish. The historical record, however, offers a more ambiguous lesson than this comforting assumption would lead us to expect. Prosperous societies have at times tolerated, and even embraced, authoritarian rule, while fragile democracies have arisen and endured in conditions of considerable hardship. What seems to matter is less the level of wealth a society has attained than the strength of the institutions and habits that hold power accountable. Where these are absent, prosperity may simply enrich those who already dominate, leaving the many no freer than before. The lesson, if there is one, is that liberty depends less on the wealth a people commands than on the arrangements that determine who may wield it and to what end.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The romantic image of the lone genius, toiling in isolation until a flash of inspiration delivers a finished masterpiece, has proved remarkably durable despite mounting evidence that it bears little resemblance to how creative work is actually accomplished. Most significant achievements, on closer inspection, turn out to be the product of long collaboration, of ideas borrowed and adapted, of countless small refinements contributed by many hands over many years. To attribute them to a single mind is not merely inaccurate; it does a quiet injustice to all those whose contributions, less visible or less celebrated, were nonetheless indispensable to the result that posterity remembers under one famous name. The myth of solitary genius, however inspiring, thus distorts our understanding of how anything of lasting value actually comes to be made.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Among the more troubling developments of recent years has been the erosion of any shared sense of what counts as a credible source of information. Where citizens once disagreed about how to interpret a body of facts they nonetheless held in common, they now increasingly inhabit separate informational worlds, each furnished with its own version of reality. This fragmentation, accelerated by algorithms that feed users whatever holds their attention, poses a peculiar threat to democratic life, which depends on the possibility of reasoned argument across lines of disagreement. When there is no longer any agreement even about the basic facts, the very ground on which such argument might proceed begins to disappear. A community that can no longer reason together about a shared reality is left with little but the bare assertion of rival convictions, each impervious to the other.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The proposition that punishment deters crime, often invoked to justify ever harsher penalties, turns out on examination to be far less straightforward than its proponents assume. Studies repeatedly indicate that what discourages potential offenders is not so much the severity of the sentence they might face as the likelihood of being caught at all. A would-be thief who believes he will escape detection is scarcely restrained by the knowledge that those who are apprehended receive long terms of imprisonment. If this is so, then policies that pour resources into lengthening sentences, while neglecting the more mundane work of effective enforcement, may be addressing the problem at precisely the wrong end. The appetite for severity, however emotionally satisfying, can thus distract a society from the measures that would actually make it safer.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The widespread enthusiasm for measuring intelligence by means of a single numerical score, however convenient for the purposes of comparison and selection, rests on a questionable premise about the nature of the human mind. It assumes that the diverse capacities we lump together under the name of intelligence, the ability to reason abstractly, to grasp another's feelings, to create, to adapt to unfamiliar situations, can all be ranged along one dimension and reduced to a point upon it. Yet there is no compelling reason to believe that these abilities, which often vary independently of one another, form so unified a quantity. A person gifted in one respect may be quite ordinary in another, and to collapse such a profile into a single figure is to discard precisely the information that makes each mind distinctive.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The conviction that the past was a simpler and happier age, from which the modern world represents a regrettable decline, exercises a powerful hold on the imagination of every generation. Such nostalgia, comforting though it may be, tends to rest upon a selective memory that magnifies the virtues of bygone times while conveniently forgetting their hardships and cruelties. The very people who lament the loss of an idealized past would, if transported back to it, find themselves shocked by the deprivations they had taken so lightly. To understand history honestly is to resist the seductive but distorting glow that distance casts upon what is gone. Only by recovering the full texture of earlier lives, their fears and discomforts no less than their pleasures, can we judge fairly whether anything of real value has indeed been lost. The honest historian, in this respect, is the enemy of comfortable myth.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is sometimes argued that the function of education is not to fill the mind with information, which any reference work can supply on demand, but to cultivate the disciplined habits of thought by which information is critically weighed and put to use. On this view, a curriculum crammed with facts to be memorized and reproduced may actually defeat the deeper purpose it is meant to serve. What a student most needs to acquire is not a stock of answers but the capacity to ask searching questions, to recognize a flawed argument, and to hold conclusions provisionally, revising them as fresh evidence comes to light. These are dispositions, not data, and they cannot be downloaded. They are cultivated slowly, through practice and example, in the kind of patient apprenticeship that no shortcut has yet been found to replace.

keyTerms: cultivate→培养; disciplined habits of thought→严谨的思维习惯; a stock of answers→一套现成答案; searching questions→深入的问题; provisionally→暂定地/姑且

tasks.sourceTextEn: The assumption that giving people more choices invariably makes them better off, so deeply ingrained in the culture of consumer societies, has been called into question by a growing body of psychological research. Beyond a certain point, it appears, an expanding array of options ceases to liberate and begins instead to oppress, burdening the chooser with the labour of comparison and the gnawing fear of having decided wrongly. Those confronted with too many alternatives often end up less satisfied with what they finally select, haunted by the alternatives they declined. Freedom of choice, it seems, is a genuine good only within limits that the prevailing ideology is curiously reluctant to acknowledge. Beyond those limits, what is offered as an expansion of our liberty may turn out, on closer inspection, to be a subtle new form of burden.

tasks.sourceTextEn: Nowhere is the gap between intention and outcome more vividly illustrated than in the long history of well-meaning attempts to reform societies from above. Reformers possessed of a clear and compelling vision have repeatedly underestimated the complexity of the systems they sought to remake, and the capacity of those systems to respond in ways no one had foreseen. Measures introduced to relieve one hardship have bred others perhaps more severe; institutions designed to embody noble principles have hardened into instruments of the very oppression they were meant to end. None of this counsels passivity in the face of injustice, but it does urge a sober awareness of how easily our best efforts may miscarry. The wise reformer, accordingly, proceeds with humility, alert to the possibility that the remedy may prove worse than the ailment it was meant to cure.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The popular image of scientists as wholly objective observers, dispassionately gathering data and following wherever the evidence leads, captures an ideal that the actual practice of science only imperfectly attains. Researchers, being human, approach their work with expectations and commitments that can subtly colour what they notice and how they interpret it. The strength of science lies not in the supposed perfection of individual investigators, who are as fallible as anyone else, but in a communal process of mutual criticism through which errors are, over time, exposed and corrected. It is this social mechanism, rather than any superhuman impartiality on the part of its practitioners, that lends scientific knowledge its distinctive reliability. Take away the open exchange of criticism, and what remains is not a purer science but a collection of unchecked individual opinions.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The growing reliance on automated systems to make decisions once entrusted to human judgment, from approving loans to recommending sentences, raises questions that we have barely begun to address. Such systems are often praised for their consistency and their freedom from the prejudices that notoriously afflict human deciders. Yet they may simply reproduce, beneath a veneer of mathematical objectivity, the very biases embedded in the historical data on which they were trained. Worse still, the workings of these systems are frequently so opaque that those affected by their verdicts cannot discover, let alone contest, the reasoning behind a decision that may profoundly alter the course of their lives. A justice that cannot be questioned, however efficient it may appear, is difficult to distinguish from no justice at all.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is a commonplace of contemporary discussion that we live in an age of accelerating change, in which the skills acquired in youth are rendered obsolete with a speed unknown to earlier generations. This observation, repeated until it has acquired the status of an unquestioned truth, may nonetheless deserve a degree of skepticism. People in every era have been inclined to regard their own moment as uniquely turbulent, and to look back upon the past as comparatively static. Whether the changes we are living through are genuinely more rapid than those that transformed, say, the lives of our great-grandparents is a question not easily settled, and one that our very immersion in the present makes peculiarly hard to judge. We are, in a sense, the least reliable witnesses to the pace of our own times.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The conviction that translation between languages is, in principle, a perfectly achievable task, requiring no more than the substitution of equivalent words, dissolves on contact with the realities of the translator's craft. Every language carries with it a freight of associations, connotations, and rhythms that resist transfer into another tongue without remainder. A word that seems on the surface to have an exact counterpart may evoke, in its native setting, a web of feeling for which the supposed equivalent provides no parallel. The translator is thus condemned to a series of compromises, forever weighing fidelity to the letter against fidelity to a spirit that no dictionary can capture. That the task can never be performed to perfection does not, however, make it any less worth attempting; it is precisely in the struggle to bridge what cannot fully be bridged that the art of translation consists.

tasks.sourceTextEn: There is something paradoxical in the way modern medicine, for all its undoubted triumphs over disease, has in some respects made the experience of dying more difficult than it once was. Where death formerly came swiftly and was accepted as an unavoidable feature of the human lot, it can now be postponed, sometimes for years, by interventions of remarkable sophistication. Yet a life thus prolonged is not always a life its possessor would have wished to lead, and the very capacity to defer the inevitable confronts patients and families with agonizing choices that earlier generations were spared. What medicine has conferred with one hand, it has in this sense complicated with the other. The power to extend life, it turns out, has obliged us to confront questions about its proper end that earlier ages, for better or worse, were never compelled to ask.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is tempting to believe that moral progress, like progress in science, accumulates steadily over time, so that each generation inherits a more enlightened set of values than the one before. The historical record offers some support for this hopeful narrative, in the gradual abolition of practices once widely condoned. Yet it would be rash to assume that such advances are irreversible, or that the moral insights painfully won by our predecessors are secure against erosion. Values sustained by habit and unexamined assumption can prove surprisingly fragile when the circumstances that nourished them change, and a society that takes its decency for granted may discover, too late, how much vigilance its preservation required. The gains of one generation, in short, are never simply bequeathed to the next; they must be actively defended, lest they quietly slip away.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The notion that a work of literature has a single, determinate meaning, waiting to be uncovered by a sufficiently attentive reader, has been challenged by a tradition of criticism that locates meaning instead in the encounter between text and interpreter. On this account, what a poem or a novel signifies is never wholly fixed by the author's intention, however carefully reconstructed, but emerges anew in each act of reading, shaped by the experiences and assumptions the reader brings to it. This does not reduce interpretation to mere caprice, since some readings remain demonstrably better supported by the text than others; it suggests, rather, that meaning is less a property residing in words than an event that words and readers together bring about.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The widely held view that emotion and reason stand opposed to one another, the former a source of error to be mastered by the latter, has been undermined by recent work in the cognitive sciences. Far from being an enemy of sound judgment, emotion appears to be indispensable to it. Patients whose capacity for feeling has been impaired by injury to certain regions of the brain, though their reasoning powers remain intact, prove strangely incapable of making the ordinary decisions that daily life requires, paralyzed by an inability to attach value to the alternatives before them. Reason, it seems, cannot function on its own; it needs emotion to tell it what matters. The old ideal of a purely rational deliberation, stripped of all feeling, thus turns out to describe not the highest form of judgment but a kind of incapacity.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The expectation that an increase in material wealth would bring a corresponding increase in human contentment has not, on the whole, been borne out by experience. Beyond the level at which basic needs are securely met, additional income appears to contribute surprisingly little to lasting happiness, a finding that sits awkwardly with the assumptions on which much of economic policy continues to be built. Part of the explanation may lie in our remarkable capacity to grow accustomed to our circumstances, so that gains which once delighted us are soon taken for granted and cease to register. We are, it seems, forever adapting to whatever we have, and forever wanting a little more. If contentment recedes as fast as we approach it, then a life organized wholly around the accumulation of more may be a pursuit with no natural resting place.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is frequently maintained that the proliferation of specialized expertise, while it has vastly extended our collective command of the natural world, has come at the cost of a fragmentation of knowledge that no individual can any longer hope to overcome. The scholar who once might have surveyed the whole field of learning has given way to the specialist, master of a narrow domain but a stranger to all that lies beyond it. Whether this development is to be lamented or welcomed remains a matter of dispute. Some see in it an impoverishment of the cultivated mind; others reply that the depth modern problems demand could never be attained by minds that insisted on ranging across everything. Perhaps the genuine challenge is not to resist specialization but to build the bridges across disciplines that no single specialist can construct alone.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The belief that human beings are by nature inclined to violence, civilization serving merely as a thin and precarious restraint upon impulses that would otherwise run riot, has exerted a powerful influence on political thought. Yet anthropologists who have studied a wide range of human societies report that organized aggression, far from being a constant of our species, varies enormously from one culture to another, and is in some all but absent. If our capacity for cruelty is undeniable, so too is our capacity for cooperation and compassion, and which of these predominates appears to depend less on some fixed feature of our nature than on the institutions and norms within which we are reared. If that is so, then the project of building a more peaceful world is not a vain struggle against nature but a realistic, if demanding, endeavour.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The supposition that privacy is a value of merely instrumental worth, important only insofar as it shields us from tangible harms such as fraud or discrimination, fails to capture the deeper significance it holds in human life. To be perpetually observed, even by those who wish us no ill, is to lose the space in which a self can be freely formed, experimenting with possibilities it is not yet prepared to defend before the world. A person who knows that every word and gesture may be recorded and scrutinized comes, almost imperceptibly, to police her own behaviour, narrowing the range of what she allows herself to think and feel. Privacy, on this understanding, is less a shield than a precondition of authentic selfhood.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It has long been assumed that competition is the surest engine of human achievement, the spur without which individuals would lapse into complacency and effort would slacken. There is evidently some truth in this, yet it is far from the whole story. A great deal of what human beings have accomplished, in the arts no less than in the sciences, has sprung not from the desire to outdo a rival but from a disinterested absorption in the task itself, a fascination that asks for no audience and seeks no prize. To organize a society as though rivalry were the only reliable motive is to neglect, and perhaps to stifle, some of the richest sources of human creativity. A culture that rewards only those who defeat others may, without realizing it, drive out the quiet, intrinsic delight from which its finest work has always sprung.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The conviction that what is natural must therefore be good, and what is artificial correspondingly suspect, exerts a curious pull on contemporary sensibilities, despite the ease with which it can be shown to collapse under scrutiny. Nature, after all, is the source not only of nourishment and beauty but of disease, famine, and countless forms of suffering that human ingenuity has labored to overcome. Conversely, much of what we rightly cherish, from medicine to music, is artificial in the precise sense of being a product of deliberate human contrivance. To invest the word natural with automatic approval is to mistake a rhetorical habit for a reasoned principle, and to let a vague feeling do the work that argument should perform.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The widespread belief that great works of art testify to the timeless and universal character of human experience, speaking across the centuries to all who encounter them, captures a genuine truth while obscuring an equally important one. It is undeniable that certain creations continue to move audiences far removed from the circumstances of their making. Yet the meaning such works hold for us is never simply identical to the meaning they bore for their original public; each generation, approaching them from the vantage of its own preoccupations, discovers in them something its predecessors could not have seen. Their endurance, paradoxically, owes as much to this capacity for reinterpretation as to any fixed and unchanging essence. A masterpiece survives, one might say, not by remaining forever the same but by proving inexhaustibly open to being seen anew.

tasks.sourceTextEn: There is a tendency, deeply rooted in the way we tell stories about our own lives, to impose upon the disordered flux of events a coherence and a direction that they did not in fact possess at the time. Looking back, we readily perceive the steps that led us to where we now stand as forming a meaningful sequence, each one a preparation for what followed. Yet this sense of inevitability is largely an illusion conferred by hindsight; the path that appears, in retrospect, to have been laid out before us was in reality assembled from choices that might easily have gone otherwise, and from accidents over which we exercised no control whatever. To recognize this is not to deny that our lives have meaning, but to locate that meaning in the stories we tell rather than in any destiny inscribed in the events themselves.

tasks.sourceTextEn: The assumption that an action is morally justified so long as it produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number, however appealing in its apparent simplicity, encounters formidable objections when its implications are pressed. For it seems to permit, and even to require, the sacrifice of an innocent individual whenever doing so would sufficiently benefit the majority, a conclusion that strikes most people as monstrous. The difficulty suggests that our moral intuitions are not exhausted by considerations of aggregate welfare, and that there are constraints on how persons may be treated which no calculation of collective advantage is allowed to override. What these constraints are, and how they are to be grounded, remains among the deepest questions of ethics. That we cannot yet answer them with confidence is no reason to pretend that the simple arithmetic of happiness can be made to bear the whole weight of morality.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It is frequently observed that the modern city, for all the opportunities it affords, can be a place of peculiar loneliness, in which one may dwell for years among multitudes and yet remain a stranger to almost everyone. The very density that throws so many lives into proximity seems, paradoxically, to discourage the kind of sustained acquaintance from which community is built. Confronted daily with more faces than the mind can hold, the city-dweller learns a protective indifference, a habit of not seeing, that shields him from sensory overload but at the cost of a certain warmth. What is gained in stimulation and freedom is thus offset, for many, by a quiet hunger for connection that the crowd does nothing to satisfy.

referencePoints: difficultSentences: 句1 the modern city, for all the opportunities it affords, can be a place of peculiar loneliness, in which one may dwell for years among multitudes and yet remain a stranger 含 for all 让步 + 省略关系词定语 + in which 定语从句；句2 The very density that throws so many lives into proximity seems...to discourage the kind of sustained acquaintance from which community is built 含 that 定语从句 + from which 定语从句；句3 Confronted daily with more faces than the mind can hold, the city-dweller learns a protective indifference, a habit of not seeing, that shields him...but at the cost of... 含过去分词状语 + 比较 + 同位语 + that 定语从句；末句 What is gained...is thus offset...by a quiet hunger for connection that the crowd does nothing to satisfy 含 what 主语从句 + that 定语从句
keyTerms: the modern city→现代城市; peculiar loneliness→特有的孤独; sustained acquaintance→持久的相识; protective indifference→保护性的冷漠; a hunger for connection→对连接的渴求
syntacticFocus: for all 让步、省略关系词定语、in which/from which/that 定语从句、过去分词作状语、比较结构、what 主语从句
meaningUnits: 常言现代城市尽管予人机遇却可能是特有孤独之所、人可于人群中居数年而对几乎所有人仍是陌生人；把众多生命投于毗邻的密度本身悖论地似阻碍据以建立共同体的持久相识；每日面对超出心智所容的面孔、城居者学得保护性冷漠、一种不去看的习惯、护其免于感官过载却以某种温暖为代价；所得之刺激与自由因而对许多人被人群无力满足的对连接的静默渴求所抵消

tasks.sourceTextEn: The notion that a nation's history can be neatly divided into discrete periods, each with its own distinctive character, is a convenience of the textbook that bears only a loose relation to the way change actually unfolds. The boundaries we draw between one age and the next, however useful for purposes of instruction, are imposed in retrospect upon a continuous and untidy flow, in which the new emerges gradually from the old and the two coexist for generations before the transition is complete. To those who lived through them, the great turning points so confidently marked by later historians would often have been invisible, indistinguishable from the ordinary succession of one year by another. The neat divisions of the textbook, useful though they are, must therefore be handled as the convenient fictions they ultimately remain.

meaningUnits: 一国历史可被整齐分为各具特征的分立时期之念是教科书的便利、与变化实际展开方式仅松散相关；我们在一代与下一代间所划界限无论于教学多有用都是事后强加于连续而不整齐之流、其中新者渐自旧者生出、二者并存数代方完成过渡；对身历者而言后世史家如此自信标出的重大转折点常本不可见、与一年接一年的寻常更替无从区分

tasks.sourceTextEn: The expectation that the spread of education would by itself dissolve prejudice and usher in an era of mutual understanding has proved, on the evidence of the past century, to be touchingly naive. Knowledge, it turns out, is no guarantee of goodwill; the same intellectual gifts that can be enlisted in the service of tolerance can be turned, with equal facility, to the construction of elaborate rationalizations for cruelty. What determines whether learning ennobles or corrupts is not the quantity of information a mind contains but the purposes to which that information is bent, purposes that are shaped by forces lying largely outside the schoolroom. Education may furnish the instruments of thought; it cannot dictate the ends they serve. Whether a trained mind becomes a force for understanding or for harm depends, in the last analysis, on a moral formation that no syllabus by itself can guarantee.

tasks.sourceTextEn: It has become almost a reflex to suppose that whatever is older must be inferior to what has replaced it, as though the mere passage of time guaranteed improvement in every domain of human activity. In technology, where each innovation builds demonstrably upon its predecessors, the assumption may often hold. But it is rash to extend it uncritically to realms such as art, morality, or the conduct of life, where there is no comparable accumulation and where much that earlier ages understood has been not surpassed but simply forgotten. The conceit that we are wiser than those who came before us, merely because we come after them, is one against which a thoughtful person does well to remain on guard.

referencePoints: difficultSentences: 句1 It has become almost a reflex to suppose that whatever is older must be inferior to what has replaced it, as though the mere passage of time guaranteed improvement 含形式主语 + that 宾语从句 + whatever/what 名词性从句 + as though 虚拟；句2 In technology, where each innovation builds demonstrably upon its predecessors, the assumption may often hold 含 where 定语从句；句3 realms such as art, morality, or the conduct of life, where there is no comparable accumulation and where much that earlier ages understood has been not surpassed but simply forgotten 含两个 where 定语从句 + that 定语从句 + not...but；末句 The conceit that we are wiser..., merely because..., is one against which a thoughtful person does well to remain on guard 含 that 同位语从句 + because 状语 + against which 定语从句
keyTerms: a reflex→一种本能反应; inferior to→劣于; the passage of time→时间的流逝; the conduct of life→生活的处世之道; the conceit→自负/妄念
syntacticFocus: it has become 形式主语、that 宾语从句、whatever/what 名词性从句、as though 虚拟、where 定语从句、that/against which 定语从句、not...but
meaningUnits: 凡更旧者必劣于取代之者之设想几成本能反应、仿佛仅时间流逝即保证人类各活动领域皆有改进；在每项创新明显建于前者之上的技术中此假设或常成立；但不加批判地推及艺术、道德、处世等无可比累积、且前代所悟许多非被超越而仅被遗忘之域则失之轻率；仅因后于前人便自以为更智的妄念是审慎者应常加提防者

tasks.sourceTextEn: The widespread faith that conflicts of value can always, in principle, be resolved by an appeal to reason, so that a sufficiently rational discussion would bring all disputants to agreement, may underestimate the genuine plurality of human ends. There are goods that cannot be jointly maximized, liberty and equality being the most familiar example, such that the pursuit of one beyond a certain point comes at the expense of the other. In such cases what is required is not the discovery of a single right answer, which may not exist, but a reasoned and humane choice among competing values, none of which can claim an absolute priority over the rest. To deny this is to mistake the character of much that is most difficult in political life.
